text: Okay, so we've covered a fair amount of academic data related to the modern aspects of bariatric surgery. We've talked a little bit about the advantages of robotic surgery in bariatrics, and we just thought we'd like to show you a live gastric bypass case.

Now, this particular procedure has been pretty heavily edited. The actual live procedure took a little bit on the long side of an hour. neighborhood of an hour and fifteen minutes. This edited version is going to take approximately thirty minutes to view.
The surgeon infiltrates the surgical site with Expirel, a long-acting local anesthetic, using a syringe.
The surgeon places a liver retractor to lift the liver away from the stomach, providing clear access to the surgical field for the gastric bypass procedure.
The surgeon rolls the omentum upwards towards the patient's head, revealing the ligament of Treitz.
The surgeon docks the robotic instruments, including a Cartier grasper visible on the right side of the screen, using two Cartiers to manipulate the intestine.
The surgeon measures the Roux limb while using Cartier graspers, with a visible skid mark created by one of the graspers.

The surgeon measures the biliary limb approximately twenty centimeters downstream from the ligament of Treitz, assessing the small bowel mesentery for sufficient length and flexibility to reach the upper abdomen.
The assisting surgeon performs blunt dissection on the mesentery to create a defect, while the robotic stapler is maneuvered into view for the next step of the procedure.
I love using the robot stapler because this device is controlled by the surgeon. It doesn't give you that awkward feeling of the surgeon being out of control.

The robotic stapler divides the small intestine, separating the biliary limb on the left from the roux limb on the right, which will later attach to the gastric pouch. The harmonic scalpel is introduced into the surgical field.

The harmonic scalpel divides the mesentery, allowing the roux limb to be positioned to reach the upper abdomen. White material fills the screen, likely indicating surgical gauze or other protective material.
That's basically steam condensation with probably just some little tiny bits of tissue causing them to coalesce and form this snow-like appearance on the screen.
The surgeon measures the Roux limb, extending it approximately one hundred fifty centimeters downstream, marking the completion of this step in the gastric bypass procedure.
The video shows the common channel on the right and the Roux limb entering the area. The grasper on the left holds the biliary limb, and a visible skid mark is present on the tissue.

We're gonna incorporate that into the anastomosis so it's not a leak risk later on. And happily, that skid mark's very, very uncommon. Just wanna show you guys all the realistic aspects of surgery, the good and the bad.

The surgeon uses a vial suture to align the small bowel for anastomosis, employing robotic instruments that provide enhanced flexibility for suturing. The video shows the complete sequence of placing the needle and tying the interrupted suture.
The surgeon places two alignment sutures using Vicryl, followed by the use of a harmonic scalpel to create the enterotomies. A single-layer hand-sewn anastomosis is performed.

To me, this is actually one of the huge advantages of robotic gastric bypass. Laparoscopically, we've come to accept the stapled anastomosis, which is quite large in caliber. The thing is that over time this stapled anastomosis can dilate and either develop into susception or perhaps even low-grade obstruction as the caliber of the wall and the subsequent wall tension begins to exceed the ability of the

The video shows the biliary limb on the left and the common channel on the right. The small anastomosis is visible, facilitating the transfer of biliary and pancreatic secretions from the biliary limb to the common channel, where they mix with food.
The surgeon creates enterotomies approximately one centimeter in length, matching the short side of a harmonic scalpel blade, and proceeds with a single-layer acrylonastomosis.

In addition to having a nice small anastomosis, I think that the sutured anastomosis is much less likely to bleed after the surgery is complete. I think all of us who have done a lot of laparoscopic gastric bypass have seen cases where the case seems to go very nicely, and of course we look intraluminally at the time that we fire the stapler. There's no bleeding. We give our patient the typical anticoagulants after surgery. and they show up overnight with a significant intraluminal bleed, later to have fairly massive bloody stools.

I think that the sutured anastomosis really prevents that staple line bleeding intraluminally, and in my experience now of about a hundred and fifty laparoscopic, I beg your pardon, about a hundred and fifty robotic gastric bypasses using hand-sewn techniques, I've had no intraluminal bleeds at all. I've been very happy with that.

The surgeon places far corner sutures using Vicryl suture material to run the back wall of the anastomosis. The robotic system allows for precise placement, ensuring full capture of the tissue layers.
The surgeon uses the robotic system to precisely place sutures at the near corner of the anastomosis, demonstrating the flexibility and accuracy of needle placement in this area.

The surgeon uses a robotic system to perform suturing with a Vicryl suture, noting that the suture appears slightly loose for efficiency. The surgeon pulls the suture through on every other throw to achieve the desired tension on the tissue. A backhand stitch is executed using the robot's flexible arms, demonstrating precision that is challenging to achieve laparoscopically.

I like the Vipral Suture because it's durable. It handles the grasping trauma that the robot does put on the suture. Never had one of these Vipral Sutures break unless there was something clear that I did wrong with the robot force.

I personally have not had experience with the barbed sutures on the gastrointestinal tract. I know a number of good surgeons who do use those materials and they don't seem to have any particular problems with the barbed suture on the GI tract. Once again, following through with every other suture, you'll see that little suture loop pull down and that's a nice signal knowing that we've achieved the proper tension without having the actual tactile feedback.
The surgeon uses the stereoscopic robot visualization to guide an SH needle into the far corner of the single-layer jejunal anastomosis, ensuring secure suturing to prevent leakage.

The surgeon completes the back row of sutures on the jejunal anastomosis and proceeds to run the front row. After placing the suture, one side is cut, and the suture is tied to another suture, securing the anastomosis.
The needle is set aside on the left part of the screen. The assistant, Alan Alvarez, delivers a 2-0 silk suture for the mesentery and retrieves two Vicryl needles.

thorough careful redundant system for confirming that all needles are out as the case progresses so that we don't have any surprises with counts being off or heaven forbid a retained suture at the end of the recovery. We do think it's super important to close the small bowel mesenteric defect. Our own experience has been that we need to use a non-absorbable suture for this location. And we think that the silk with its mild to moderate inflammatory reaction is actually beneficial at this site because we want to stimulate a little bit of inflammation to generate some beneficial adhesions to keep this area closed. In this aspect as well, I've been very happy with my robot cases. I've not seen any internal hernias in my one or fifty or so gastric bypass cases so far.

The surgeon completes the closure of the small bowel mesentery defect using a retrocholic, retrogastric technique. The procedure begins just anterior to the ligament of Treitz, with the defect opened in an avascular plane. The robotic system provides enhanced visualization of the surgical field.

The surgeon uses the left-hand grasper to hold the back wall of the stomach while passing the rulem cephalad behind the colon into the retrogastric space. The colon and omentum are rolled towards the feet, bringing the stomach back into view.

The surgeon refocuses on the gastrohepatic ligament, opening it widely to isolate the lesser curve neurovascular bundle. The division of this bundle provides clear visualization of the stomach pouch formation and facilitates the mobilization of the bypass stomach for the rulem later in the procedure.
The surgeon uses a robotic stapler to fire a transverse blue load, aligning the vertical staple loads to form the gastric pouch just distal to the esophageal fat pad.

The surgeon identifies the anterior border of the left cruce and dissects the high posterior short gastric vessel towards the patient's left. Using the robotic Cartier, the stomach is pulled down slightly from the left cruce before positioning the stapler for the vertical division of the gastric pouch.

The surgeon uses robotic stapler loads to divide the stomach, with three to five staple loads being applied to achieve complete division. The robotic system provides enhanced visualization and precision during this step.

The surgeon uses the robotic stapler to perform dissection and grasping, similar to laparoscopic techniques. A white staple load is selected to compress the remaining bridge of tissue, minimizing staple line bleeding.

The surgeon has completed the separation of the gastric segments, creating a small, isolated gastric pouch. The distal bypass stomach is moved downwards to locate the roux limb in the retrogastric space, which is then brought up for further surgical manipulation.

The surgeon carefully aligns the roux limb to prevent a corkscrew anastomosis, using the screen right stapler arm for support. Posterior alignment sutures are placed to secure the roux limb, while an orogastric tube is advanced down the esophagus by the anesthesia team.

The orogastric tube is visible, serving as a guide for the placement of the posterior running suture. The surgeon prepares to use a two-ovigral running suture to align the back wall, followed by creating enterotomies and performing a running internal layer anastomosis between the cut edges of the stomach and the roux limb. An interrupted front layer vicral anastomosis will be completed.

The surgeon completes the back wall alignment of the gastric pouch and prepares to create a gastrotomy by excising the gastric staple line. A generous-sized gastrojejunal anastomosis is formed to minimize the risk of postoperative stricture.

Back to the excision of the gastric staple line, in my opinion, or I should say better, in my experience, that's become important because when I make a fairly long anastomosis, if I create my gastrotomy immediately adjacent to the staple line, either posterior or anterior, in my experience and especially using the Firefly vascular visualization option I've seen some ischemia at that location and in my first fifty or so robot gastric bypasses I had two patients who had fairly significant and very symptomatic gastric ulcers at this anastomosis and one of those ladies actually had to have a revision done for that in the last hundred cases or so since changing technique with excision of the staple line, having excellent perfusion on both sides of the anastomosis. I haven't had any subsequent ulcers.

Similar to the belief I have about reduced bleeding at the anastomosis using a sutured rather than a staple technique, I've not had any bleeding from this gastro-degenerative anastomosis where using the staple technique I did have the occasional intraluminal bleed with hematemesis one, two, three hours after the surgical procedure.

The surgeon places sutures at the far corner of the anastomosis, ensuring both are positioned before tying to maintain clear visibility of the tissue planes. The first suture is tied at the far corner, with an additional adjustment made to align the back wall for subsequent handling.
The surgeon performs a backhand suture on the jejunal side using two robotic needle drivers, demonstrating left-hand suturing technique for the gastroduodenal anastomosis.

The surgeon uses a large needle driver in the left hand and a mega suture cut in the right hand to perform suturing. Vicryl suture is utilized for its ability to withstand the robot's grasping force without losing tensile strength. The surgeon carefully lines up the initial suture bites and pulls the suture tight on every other run to ensure proper tension is applied to the tissue.

The surgeon pulls the suture through, observing the tension on the tissue at the near corner of the anastomosis. The backhand suturing is performed under excellent visualization, showing a well-sized anastomosis coming together without tension.
The surgeon completes the sutured approximation of the far corner of the anastomosis, ensuring a tiny gastric pouch is created without additional length, minimizing tension on the anastomosis.

The surgeon adjusts the stabilization grasper to enhance visualization of the surgical field. The orogastric tube is advanced into the gastric pouch, visible as it enters the anastomosis and is guided across into the roux limb. This maneuver ensures the anastomosis remains open without inadvertently closing the back wall.

The surgeon prepares for a methylene blue pressure test to check for leaks, with the orogastric tube pre-positioned for the test. The front wall of the anastomosis is nearly complete, and the surgeon makes small camera adjustments to enhance visualization during the procedure.

Dr. Alvarez, working at the bedside, uses a large Babcock clamp to occlude the outflow from the Roux limb. The Roux limb inflates with methylene blue, indicating pressure buildup. The surgeon visually inspects the lesser and greater curved sides of the anastomosis for leaks. After confirming no leaks, interrupted anti-tension sutures are placed to cover the corners and middle front wall of the anastomosis.

The surgeon completes the gastrojejunal anastomosis, ensuring the inner layer is secure. Sutures are placed, causing the anastomosis to roll slightly inward. The needle is retrieved, and the colon and omentum are rolled back towards the head. The procedure is performed in a reverse Trendelenburg position.

One of the concerns that I had before doing this robotically was I was worried about needing to reposition the patient during the surgical procedure, which we commonly do laparoscopically. And it's really not that much of a problem to do both parts, or I should say the entire surgical technique in the reverse Trendelenburg position.

The surgeon closes the Peterson defect using Tua silk sutures, aiming to create mild inflammation for beneficial adhesions. Following this, three interrupted sutures are placed to secure the Roux limb to the transverse mesocolon, preventing movement in that area.

The surgeon completes the three-suture technique to prevent herniation adjacent to the roux limb, securing the roux limb serosa to the transverse mesocolon with a strong fixation point. The gastrojejunal anastomosis is inspected, showing no bleeding and a well-positioned anastomosis.
